coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of folic acid-based carbon dots for solar photocatalytic sulfamethazine degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Bakier, Yasmin M.
El-Bery, Haitham M.
coredata.dc:description: The presence of sulfamethazine (SMZ) in the environment poses significant risks to both ecological systems and human health, necessitating the development of efficient degradation methods. This study presents a novel approach for synthesizing green carbon dots (G-CDs) using expired folic acid tablets as a single precursor through a one-pot hydrothermal process for the photodegradation of SMZ, which has not been reported before. Notably, green-synthesized carbon dots (G-CDs) exhibited superior performance compared to those synthesized from pure folic acid (FA-CDs) toward SMZ photodegradation. The results demonstrated that under simulated sunlight irradiation, G-CDs achieved over 94% degradation of SMZ within 75 min, while FA-CDs only degraded around 10% of SMZ. Full SMZ photodegradation was realized under optimal conditions. Further investigation into the kinetics and photogenerated charge carriers revealed that the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of SMZ was mainly due to holes, hydroxyl radical and superoxide anions. These findings emphasize the potential of green-synthesized G-CDs as highly efficient photocatalysts for environmental remediation applications, particularly in the degradation of organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Folic acid-based carbon dots
Hydrothermal treatment
Sulfamethazine (SMZ) photocatalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and fundamental properties of CDs
Optimizing photocatalytic degradation parameters of SMZ

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b) TEM images of the synthesized G -CDs and FA-CDs, respectively. The insets of (a) and (b) are histograms of the dots distribution. (c,d) HR-TEM images of G -CDs and FA-CDs, respectively, and the lattice spacing of the crystalline dots, with d = 0.33 nm for both samples.
(a)XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs, (b) FTIR spectra of synthesized CDs.

(a,b) XPS of full survey for FA-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. (c,d) high resolution XPS of C 1 s band for FA-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. (e,f) high resolution XPS of O 1 s band for FA-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. (g,h) high resolution XPS of N 1 s band for FA-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. (i) high resolution XPS of P 2p band for G-CDs.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of the synthesized dots. (b) The normalized fluorescence spectra of FA-CDs and G-CDs at the most effective excitation wavelength for each sample individually. (c)The normalized fluorescence spectra of both samples at excitation wavelength of 380 nm. (d) Fluorescence decay data of FA-CDs and G-CDs.

(a) Photocatalytic activity of the FA-CDs and G-CDs catalysts for SMZ degradation. (b) Photocatalytic activity of G-CDs catalysts synthesized at different reaction temperatures for SMZ degradation. (c) The effect of catalyst volume on the photocatalytic activity of the optimum G-CDs sample for SMZ degradation. (d) The effect of pH on the photocatalytic activity of the optimum G-CDs sample for SMZ degradation. (e,f) The effect of irradiation by different LED wavelengths and different ranges of wavelengths from Xenon lamp, respectively, on the photocatalytic activity of the optimum G-CDs sample for SMZ degradation.

(a) The reusability of G-CDs after adjusting all the effective parameters for photocatalytic degradation of SMZ. (b,c) Effect of scavengers on the photocatalytic activity of the optimum G-CDs sample for SMZ degradation. Reaction conditions: SMT = 15 ppm; photocatalyst volume= 5 mL; pH= 11, t = 75 min.
(a, b) photocatalytic activity of the optimum G-CDs sample in tab and river water samples for SMZ degradation. Reaction conditions: SMT = 15 ppm; photocatalyst volume= 5 mL; pH= 11, t = 75 min.

(a)EIS plots of FA-CDs and G-CDs, (a) in dark and (b) under irradiation by simulated sun light. (c) CV curves of FA-CDs and G-CDs electrodes. (d)Transient photocurrent response (CAM) of FA-CDS and G-CDs electrodes under chopper light on/ off cycles. (e,f) Photocurrent-potential profiles (LSV) for FA-CDs and G-CDs.
Comparison with other Photocatalysts for degradation of SMZ.

row.entry: Bisphenol S-doped g-C3N4 nanosheets modified by boron nitride quantum dots

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of folic acid-based carbon dots for solar photocatalytic sulfamethazine degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut 71515, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Assiut University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut 71515, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut 71515, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Assiut University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Multifunctional Materials laboratory, Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut 71515, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of sulfamethazine (SMZ) in the environment poses significant risks to both ecological systems and human health, necessitating the development of efficient degradation methods. This study presents a novel approach for synthesizing green carbon dots (G-CDs) using expired folic acid tablets as a single precursor through a one-pot hydrothermal process for the photodegradation of SMZ, which has not been reported before. Notably, green-synthesized carbon dots (G-CDs) exhibited superior performance compared to those synthesized from pure folic acid (FA-CDs) toward SMZ photodegradation. The results demonstrated that under simulated sunlight irradiation, G-CDs achieved over 94% degradation of SMZ within 75 min, while FA-CDs only degraded around 10% of SMZ. Full SMZ photodegradation was realized under optimal conditions. Further investigation into the kinetics and photogenerated charge carriers revealed that the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of SMZ was mainly due to holes, hydroxyl radical and superoxide anions. These findings emphasize the potential of green-synthesized G-CDs as highly efficient photocatalysts for environmental remediation applications, particularly in the degradation of organic pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The identification of several drugs as environmental pollutants has sparked significant research interest for their removal [1–6]. When introduced into water, drugs such as antibiotics can trigger genetic and biological changes, particularly in the form of bacterial resistance, when exposed to the environment for an extended period. [7–9]. Currently, the elimination of antibiotics from the environment primarily occurs through direct photolysis in natural settings, which is a time-consuming process and may result in the formation of derivative lesions. [10]. Moreover, antibiotics comprise various compounds that can continually enter the environment and potentially harm ecosystems and human health. [11–13]. Sulfamethazine, which maintains antibacterial activity, is an antibiotic commonly utilized in both human and veterinary treatments.[14–18]. Previous studies have detected sulfamethazine in various water zones, including ground, surface, and wastewater, with concentrations ranging from 20 to 1000 ng/L. [19]. With the increasing use of antibiotics in daily life, it is imperative to develop an efficient, fast, and straightforward method for water purification. Semiconductor photocatalysis presents a promising solution as it is a cost-effective and eco-friendly technology commonly employed to remove antibiotics from water. [20–24]. Carbon dots (CDs) are increasingly preferred over semiconductor quantum dots for use in various fields due to their advantages, including biocompatibility, chemical inertness, non-toxicity, low cost, and easy synthesis procedures.[25,26]. Over the past few years, there has been significant interest in the use of carbon dots (CDs) for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. CDs can fulfill multiple roles within photocatalyst systems, serving as independent photocatalyst materials, photosensitizers, or electron donors/acceptors. For instance, CDs with up-conversion photoluminescence properties have been applied to decorate the surfaces of wide band gap semiconducting materials like ZnO [27]. This enables the absorption of visible or near-infrared light, followed by the emission of ultraviolet photons to activate the photocatalyst system. Additionally, in this study, CDs acted as electron acceptors. Numerous studies have explored the use of CDs for photocatalytic degradation of toxic organic pollutants, including dyes and pharmaceutical substances [28,29]. Furthermore, CDs readily combine with metals, semiconductors, and other materials to enhance the activity of the catalyst system [30]. CDs can also serve as conductor for electron transfer between different semiconductors, such as g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 [31–33], resulting in enhanced indole degradation efficiency. Despite CDs possess strong light absorption capabilities across the entire solar spectrum, very few studies reported their use as independent photocatalysts [28,29]. For SMZ photodegradation, recent studies used either carbon nitride or modified graphitic carbon nitrides as a photocatalysts in the degradation process [10,34–39]. Moreover, few studies employed CDs as a cocatalyst with graphitic carbon nitrides for SMZ degradation process [40,41]. However, up to the best of our knowledge no study has utilized CDs as an independent photocatalyst for SMZ removal. Therefore, our study is the first to report the successful utilization of CDs derived from expired folic acid tablets as a highly effective photocatalyst for SMZ degradation.

Herein, we present a green-synthesized carbon dot produced via a one-pot hydrothermal process using expired folic acid tablets as a single precursor (G-CDs). We compared the properties of the resulting G-CDs to those of CDs synthesized under the same experimental conditions but using pure folic acid precursor (FA-CDs) and assessed their characteristics. Additionally, we evaluated the performance of both samples in the photodegradation of sulfamethazine and found that G-CDs degraded more than 94% of SMZ after 75 min of irradiation under stimulated sunlight, while FA-CDs only degraded about 10% of SMZ. We also investigated various parameters that may affect degradation efficiency, such as synthesis temperature, catalyst concentration, and pH, to determine the optimal conditions for SMZ photodegradation under simulated solar irradiation. Under the optimal experimental conditions, more than 94% of SMZ was degraded, and we further explored the most effective wavelength after examining various monochromatic LED sources. We investigated the kinetics and photogenerated charge carriers using both steady-state fluorescence investigation and a photoelectrochemical system. Finally, we conducted an in-depth investigation of the possible photocatalytic mechanism of G-CDs under stimulated sunlight irradiation.

Expired Folic acid (5 mg FA) tablets (also contains: calcium hydrogen phosphate dibasic anhydrous, lactose monohydrate, maize starch, magnesium stearate) produced by the Nile Company, Egypt. Folic acid powder was obtained with purity of 99 + % (Acros organics, Belgium). SMZ extra pure grade of 99% was received from (Star Chemie PVT. LTD., India). EDTA disodium salt, benzoquinone (BQ), sodium azide (NaN3), triethanolamine (TEOA), Isopropanol, and oxalic acid (Na2C2O4) were obtained from either Acros organics or Fisher chemical. All used chemicals were of high purity grade and used without further purification.

The hydrothermal method was used to prepare green carbon dots (G-CDs) using expired folic acid tablets as the sole ingredient, while FA chemical powder was added to prepare FA-CDs. To prepare FA-CDs, 0.045 gm of FA was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 15 min using probe sonication (750 W, VCX-750 sonics, USA) at 30% amplitude. The resulting FA liquid was then heated for 10 h at 150 °C in an oven, after being placed in a 90 mL Teflon-based stainless-steel autoclave. The synthesized FA-CDs were filtered using Whatman filter paper and subsequently centrifuged at a speed of 4000 rpm for 30 min. The same experimental conditions were applied to synthesize G-CDs at different temperatures (120 °C, 150 °C, and 180 °C), except that 9 pills of folic acid tablets (each containing 5 mg of FA as the active ingredient) were used as the single precursor. Compared to other CDs precursor FA contains lots of nitrogen (N) and functional groups of carboxyl (–CO2H), amino (–NH2), and hydroxyl (–OH). Therefore, FA has an excessive prospect for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-C-dots) using hydrothermal processes without any need for intentionally doping or passivation processes [42].

For surface and morphological structural analysis of the synthesized CDs, a High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (JSM-2100 JEOL) was utilized. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a Philips 1700 version diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA), while FTIR spectra were recorded using a Bruker Vertex 70. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using a Thermo Fisher (K-alpha) instrument with monochromatic micro-focused Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV). Optical absorption and fluorescence spectra were documented using the lambda 40 (PerkinElmer, USA) spectrophotometer and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA), respectively. Fluorescence decay spectra were measured using nanosecond time-correlated single-photon-counting (Horiba 3000 U).

A specific volume/concentration of CDs sample as a catalyst was added to 150 mL of 15 ppm SMZ dissolved in distilled water. The degradation process was carried out in a 150 mL photocatalytic cell equipped with a side irradiation Quartz window (25 mm) under continuous magnetic stirring at 700 rpm. Before irradiation, a dark response was conducted to achieve absorption-desorption equilibrium. The degradation response was tested using a 300 W Xenon lamp with AM 1.5 filter ≥ 300 (CEL-HXF300-T3, PE300BFA, CEAULIGHT, China), Vis-Xenon (equipped with a ≥ 400 nm cut filter), and various LED sources (365 nm, 400 nm, 450 nm, 460 nm, 500 nm, and 520 nm). Samples were withdrawn at stipulated time interval, and the concentration of SMZ was determined using a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system (Thermo Scientific Dionex Ultimate 3000), which included a pump (LPG-3400 SD), autosampler (WPS 3000SL Analytical), column compartment (TCC-3000 SD), and diode array detector (DAD-3000). A C18 column (TSK gel ODS-100Z column, 4.6 ×250 mm) was used at a temperature of 30 °C. The mobile phase consisted of methanol and water (45%:55%), and the flow rate was set to 1 mL•min−1, with a DAD detector acquiring results at a wavelength of 270 nm. Additionally, the photodegradation mechanism was studied by conducting experiments using various radical scavengers, including EDTA for hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and holes (h+), BQ for superoxide anion scavenger (O2-), NaN3 for electron radical (e-), TEOA for holes (h+), isopropanol for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and Na2C2O4 for superoxide radical (•O2-) [34].

To perform electrochemical characterization under light irradiation, the as-prepared CDs samples were initially loaded onto FTO (fluorine tin oxide conductive glass, Sigma-Aldrich, ca. 8 Ω/sq) using a drop-casting method. A 20 µL suspension of CDs was cast over the FTO electrode (1.5 × 2 cm2), and the process was repeated five times. The electrodes were then air-dried at room temperature. Electrochemical measurements were conducted in a three-electrode rectangular quartz cell consisting of the prepared FTO electrode as the working electrode, Pt wire as a counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and an aqueous Na2SO4 solution (0.1 M, without pH adjustment). The solution was purged with pure Argon gas (99.999%, 100 mL•min−1) for 20 min before the measurements to remove dissolved air. The working electrode was irradiated with a 300 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300-T3, PE300BFA, CEAULIGHT, China), Vis-Xenon (equipped with a ≥ 400 nm cut filter). Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) were acquired at a scan rate of 50 mV•s−1 in the range from 1 V to − 1 V using a potentiostat (CS350, CorrTest® Electrochemical Workstation, Wuhan, China). Chronoamperometry (CAM) was measured under chopped light irradiation at a fixed potential of 0.8 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out by applying 20 mV amplitude over the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz, both under visible light irradiation and in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and fundamental properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a, b displays TEM data for the synthesized G-CDs and FA-CDs, respectively. The dots in the figures indicate that the particles have a nearly monodispersed and round shape. Statistical analysis of the number of dots reveals that the average size of the G-CDs and FA-CDs is 8.5 nm and 5.5 nm, respectively (see Fig. 1 a, b inset). Additionally, Fig. 1 c, d shows high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images that show fringe lines. Analysis of the interatomic crystalline lattice spacing reveals that both the G-CDs and FA-CDs have an interatomic spacing of 0.33 nm, affirming their graphitic configuration [43].

Fig. 2 a shows that the XRD measurements confirm this conclusion, as evidenced by the 002 crystallographic plane at 24.28 degrees for FA-CDs and 22.23 degrees for G-CDs. Using the Bragg relation, we can determine the d-interlayer spacing from the XRD peaks. Analysis of the data revealed that the d-spacing for FA-CDs and G-CDs was 0.37 nm and 0.4 nm, respectively, which is longer than the d-spacing observed in graphite structures. However, the values obtained from HRTEM and XRD analyses showed some discrepancy, indicating that our synthesized dots have lattice distortions due to the presence of nitrogen atoms and surface functional groups. This will be further confirmed by the FTIR and XPS analysis shown in Fig. 2 b and 3.

Fig. 2 b displays the FTIR spectra for the FA-CDs and G-CDs. The broadest peak consists of two different peaks, with one peak corresponding to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group O-H at 3460 cm−1, and the other peak at 3387 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of the N-H group. Another peak appeared at 2080 cm−1, indicating the presence of CC, and a final peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups CO. The FTIR shows no significant difference in the functional groups between the two types of synthesized dots, with only a slight shift in the peaks for G-CDs towards longer wavenumbers compared to FA-CDs. Figs. 3 a and 3 b display the XPS full survey spectra for the FA-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. Both samples exhibit three peaks corresponding to C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s, with specific percentages for each sample indicated in Table S1. However, G-CDs display an additional peak corresponding to P 2p.

Figs. 3 c and 3 d display the high-resolution spectra of the C 1 s band for both samples. The band can be de-convoluted into three main peaks at approximately 284.23, 286, and 287 eV, which correspond to the C 1 s states in C−C/CC, C−N/C−O, and CO, respectively, for both FA-CDs and G-CDs. However, an additional peak appeared at 292.42 eV for FA-CDs, which corresponds to C−C/C-H. The O 1 s band consists of two peaks at 530.86 and 531.68 eV for CO and C-OH/C-O-C, respectively, for FA-CDs, while G-CDs deconvoluted only one peak at 532.51 eV corresponding to C-O, as shown in Figs. 3 e and 3 f. The N 1 s spectra display one band at approximately 399.3 eV for both samples, indicating the presence of pyridinic N, as shown in Fig. 3 g and 3h [44] . The HR-XPS curve of the P 2p band observed in the G-CDs full spectra can be separated into two peaks at 133.24 and 134.28 eV, corresponding to PO4 3- and C2PO2/CPO3, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 3 i [45].

UV–vis absorptions properties of FA-CDs and G-CDs were investigated. As indicated in Fig. 4 a, FA-CDs curve exhibited three apparent absorption bands beginning from 240 to 345 nm approximately. However, the curve of G-CDs showed one broad peak at 281.77 nm. The observed short-wavelength absorption bands were generally attributed to the π→π * however the long-wavelength bands were ascribed to the n→π * transitions of CDs[46,47].

The fluorescence observed in the synthesized dots containing nitrogen atoms is primarily expected to originate from the nitrogen-bonded aromatic carbon rings present on the surface and/or in the interior of the dots. Figs. S1a and S1b depict the fluorescence emission spectra of FA-CDs and G-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. Both samples exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent emission, which is a common behavior observed in the majority of synthesized CDs in literature. This behavior is attributed to the photosensitive excitation of various surface states impurities near the Fermi level of the dots, which is linked to the surface functional groups and the size of the dots [48,49]. When photoexcited at 380 nm, the strongest fluorescence intensity was observed in FA-CDs, with emission at λ = 446 nm. Conversely, G-CDs exhibited the strongest intensity when photoexcited at 420 nm, and the emission was observed at λ = 485 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 b. Additionally, both samples of CDs exhibited blue fluorescence under UV exposure, which was visible to the naked eye, indicating that both samples were well-dispersed in aqueous solution. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 4 c, when both samples were excited by a wavelength of 380 nm, they emitted light in the blue region, with FA-CDs emitting at 446 nm and G-CDs emitting at 462 nm. The fluorescence of G-CDs was observed to be shifted by approximately 20 nm towards longer (red) wavelengths, which may be attributed to the presence of phosphorus atoms in the G-CDs, as observed in the XPS spectra of the sample. The fluorescence quantum yield (QYfl-CDs) of the synthesized dots was calculated using a standard reference in water, according to the following equation: [50]:

QY
fl − CDs

QY

2
)
(1)where QY
fl-CDs is the fluorescence quantum yield of the as-synthesized dots while QY
fl-Try is the quantum yield of tryptophan as a reference, fl. represented the integrated area of the fluorescence curves, n is the refractive index of the solvent (water) for both the CDs and Tryptophan, and A. is the corresponding absorbance. The QY
fl-CDs of FA-CDs and G-CDs were calculated from Eq. 1 and found to be 46.3% and 28.7%, respectively. The high QY
fl-CDs of FA-CDs, owing to the presence of nitrogen and the surface defects on the dots. Nevertheless, The QY
fl-CDs of G-CDs is much lower due to the existence of phosphorus atoms in the sample as confirmed from XPS spectra.

Fig. 4 d gives an information about the fluorescence decay lifetimes for both samples. The decay curves illustrate an exponential decay lifetime, which can be well fitted by a bi-exponential equation

for FA-CDs and G-CDs. The values of the lifetimes are shown in Table S2. The average lifetime ˂τ˃ of excitons in the dots can be calculated from the relation:

It is noticed that the excitonic average lifetime of FA-CDs is longer than the that of the G-CDs, which confirmed the fluorescence data as the QYfl-CDs of G-CDs is lesser than that of FA-CDs (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing photocatalytic degradation parameters of SMZ
ce:section.ce:para:
We conducted photocatalytic tests on FA-CDs and G-CDs to evaluate their efficacy in removing SMZ antibiotics under simulated solar light using a Xenon lamp. Prior to photoexcitation, a mixed solution of 150 mL of 15 ppm SMZ and 5 mL of CDs was stirred adequately for 30 min in the dark to achieve dissolution and adsorption equilibrium. The solution was then irradiated, and data was collected every 10 min for 75 min to determine the degradation efficiency of the dots towards SMZ removal. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the degradation rate of SMZ was calculated to be 71.7% after 75 min when G-CDs were used as a catalyst. In contrast, the other sample (FA-CDs) exhibited a low degradation rate of approximately 10%. To quantify the degradation rates of SMZ, the data was fitted using the pseudo first-order kinetic equation:
(3)
ln (
C
/
C
0
) =
k
ap
t
where k ap (min−1) is the apparent rate constant, C0 the initial concentration of SMZ and C is the concentration after passing specific time. The values of k
ap after calculation from Fig. S2a was found to be 0.0222 min−1 and 0.00142 min−1 for G-CDs and FA-CDs, respectively. From these data, it is noticed that the green synthesized dots are more effective for the removal process of SMZ antibiotic.

Based on the previous experiment, G-CDs have shown significant potential as a catalyst for SMZ removal, and it is worthwhile to investigate their performance under different parameters, such as synthesis temperature, G-CDs concentration, pH, and various sources of illumination. To further explore this, three different samples of G-CDs were synthesized at different reaction temperatures and tested for their photocatalytic activity. Results indicated that the sample prepared at 150 °C exhibited the highest activity towards the degradation of SMZ, as depicted in Fig. 5 b and S2b. To investigate the impact of G-CDs concentration on the removal and apparent rates, varying volumes of the optimal sample synthesized at 150 °C were added to a solution of 150 mL containing 15 ppm of SMZ. The results are presented in Fig. 5 c and S2c. The optimal sample of synthesized G-CDs was subjected to pH evaluation by adding a specific volume of 5 mL of the carbon dots to the SMZ solution. Fig. 5 d and S2d demonstrate that an increase in pH value resulted in an increase in the degradation rate. At pH = 11, the degradation rate reached approximately 94%, representing a 24% increase from the rate obtained from the sample without pH adjustment. After adding 5 mL of the optimal sample of G-CDs at pH= 11, the impact of different illumination sources such as various LEDs and different ranges of Xenon lamp was investigated. The efficacy of LED sources is depicted in Figs. 5 e and 5 f, indicating that the most effective sources are 365 nm and 400 nm. Conversely, an increase in the excitation wavelength of the lighting source resulted in a decrease in the degradation rate. The superior performance of the aforementioned sources may be attributed to the photosensitive properties of the dots, as the intensities of the emission drop after being photoexcited by wavelengths greater than 430 nm. Additionally, the simulated solar spectra from the full range of Xenon lamp and the visible range from the lamp using a filter were tested, and it was found that the full range is more efficient in removing SMZ (Figs. S2e , f). Table S3 lists all the degradation rates and the apparent rates for all the experiments.

Table S3 reveals that by adjusting the parameters of the optimal synthesized sample, complete degradation of SMZ can be achieved using G-CDs as a catalyst. Adding 7 mL of the catalyst without adjusting any other parameter resulted in a degradation efficiency of over 92% after 75 min. However, under the irradiation of solar light at pH= 11, complete removal can be achieved in less than 75 min.

In addition, the reusability of the best sample of G-CDs as a catalyst was investigated by adjusting the other parameters for the reaction to ensure that it is a suitable candidate for industrial usage. In each cycle, after removing SMZ from the reactor, an equal amount of SMZ at the same concentration was added to restart the reaction without any additional treatment or amount of the used catalyst. Fig. 6 a demonstrates that G-CDs is highly stable for the reaction, with no noticeable decrease in activity even after four cycles. This indicates that the sample can be reused for the reaction and may be employed in the industrial removal of sulfamethazine antibiotics in aqueous solutions.
Recent studies about photocatalytic degradation of SMZ using different photocatalysts are summarized in Table 1.

To elucidate the photodegradation mechanism of SMZ, the photocatalytic activities were investigated in the presence of various radical scavengers by adding specific quenching agents, each representing the effect of a scavenger. Both EDTA and TEOA were utilized to explore whether the scavenging of holes (h+) affected the degradation of SMZ. Furthermore, EDTA was also used to examine the effect of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) on the degradation process. As shown in Fig. 6 b, c and Table S4, the photocatalytic activity was almost completely inhibited after the addition of TEOA or EDTA. Moreover, BQ was added to examine the influence of superoxide anion (O2-) scavenging on photocatalytic degradation, a large decrease in the results was observed. These findings suggest that h+, •OH and O2- are the primary species involved in the photodegradation of SMZ. On the other hand, the addition of NaN3, isopropanol, and Na2C2O4 as precursors, indicating the effect of electron radical (e-), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and superoxide radical (•O2-), respectively, moderately inhibited the degradation process. Thus, it is evident that the mechanism of photodegradation is secondary associated with e-, •OH, and •O2- species. Additionally, the contribution of each scavenger to the photodegradation process was calculated using the corresponding equation:
R
s
=
k
s
/
k
ap
≈
(
k
ap
−
k
i
)
/
k
ap
where R S is the fractional contribution of the corresponding scavenger, k S is the apparent rate constant contributed by the trapped scavenger and k i is the apparent rate constant for SMZ photodegradation in existence of a certain quenching agent.

To evaluate the impact of natural organic macromolecules and inorganic anions on the photocatalytic efficiency of SMZ degradation, two different water sources were utilized. Real river water samples were collected from Ibrahimiya canal, while tap water was obtained from a typical drinking water treatment plant at Assiut University. The water quality parameters of these samples are presented in Table S5. Prior to conducting the photocatalytic degradation experiment under identical optimized conditions (15 ppm SMZ, 5 mL G-CDs, pH 11, 75 min of irradiation using a 300 W xenon lamp with full spectrum), each sample was filtered using a 0.45 µm membrane. The results depicted in Fig. 7, demonstrate that both samples exhibited exceptional performance in degrading SMZ without any inhibitory effects. This outcome can be attributed to the presence of certain common anions in the surface water, such as bicarbonate and low-concentration nitrate, which enhanced the photodegradation of SMZ. On the other hand, other anions present in the water samples had a negligible impact on the degradation efficiency [40]. A similar observation can be made for natural organic macromolecules, which can be attributed to their low concentration in the river water sample. These findings further validate the remarkable efficacy of the synthesized G-CDs for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.

The complete degradation of SMZ into its mineral components can be achieved through the utilization of powerful oxidizing agents, namely h+, •OH, and O2-, as previously identified. The comprehensive mineralization process of SMZ can be represented by Eq. (3.1), wherein the organic nitrogen and sulfur compounds are transformed into inorganic ions such as nitrate, ammonium, and sulfate, along with the production of carbon dioxide and water. However, the analysis of degradation intermediates requires a more advanced technique such as LC-MS/MS. In this study, we collected the reaction mixture after 30 min of light irradiation and extracted it using chloroform and ethyl acetate. The obtained extract was then subjected to GC-MS analysis. Several compounds were identified as degradation intermediates of SMZ, and their details are summarized in Table S6. The generated oxidizing species, such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and holes (h+), produced during the photocatalytic process, can attack various functional groups within the SMZ molecule, resulting in the formation of different degradation products. Each of these degradation products underwent further oxidation until complete mineralization was achieved.
(3.1)
Image 1

Electrochemical measurements were conducted to investigate the photoelectric properties of the prepared CDs and gain a deeper understanding of their photocatalytic performance. Various techniques such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and chronoamperometry (CAM) were employed. The charge transfer dynamics of photocatalysts are crucial for their activity toward oxidation or reduction reactions. Therefore, EIS measurements were performed for the prepared CDs under both dark and light conditions. The radius of the semicircle/arc shape in the Nyquist plot (Fig. 8 a, b) is directly related to the charge transfer resistance of the photogenerated e-/h+ pairs. The smaller the semicircle radius, the higher the conductivity of the CDs. As depicted in Fig. 8a, b, G-CDs exhibited better charge dynamics than FA-CDs, as indicated by the lower semicircle radius under both dark and light conditions. The catalytic activity was measured through cyclic voltammetry testing, where a higher reduction current density (J) indicated higher catalytic activity of the sample. As illustrated in Fig. 8 c, the reduction current density of G-CDs (0.4 mA•cm−2) was almost double that of the FA-CDs sample (0.26 mA•cm−2). The transient photocurrent response of both samples was obtained at a fixed potential (1 V versus Ag/AgCl) via chronoamperometry testing. Both stability and photocatalytic activity could be assessed through this test, where the amount of photocurrent produced during the test provides valuable information about the CD's performance. As presented in Fig. 8 d, the photocurrent response reached about 30 and 18 µA•cm−2 for G-CDs and FA-CDs samples, respectively. Furthermore, the photocurrent profile was assessed versus the applied voltage using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test under dark (black line) and light (red line) conditions as shown in Fig. 8 e, f, for G-CDs and FA-CDs samples, respectively. Both samples exhibited a photocurrent response that increased with increasing the applied potential in the positive direction. The G-CDs sample showed almost double the photocurrent response compared to FA-CDs sample. Based on the aforementioned results, it can be concluded that the G-CDs sample is superior to the FA-CDs sample in terms of photocatalytic performance towards the degradation of sulfamethazine.

This study presents a novel approach for the synthesis of green carbon dots (G-CDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal process using expired folic acid tablets as a single precursor for the first time. A comparison was made with carbon dots prepared using pure folic acid powder (FA-CDs), revealing distinct characteristics for both samples. The performance of G-CDs in the photodegradation of sulfamethazine (SMZ) was found to be superior to that of FA-CDs. This study systematically analyzed factors such as the synthesis temperature of the carbon dots, catalyst concentration, and pH, while also evaluating different light sources to optimize the conditions for SMZ photodegradation using green-synthesized carbon dots. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, G-CDs achieved over 94% degradation of SMZ within 75 min, while FA-CDs only degraded around 10% of SMZ. Optimal conditions for SMZ photodegradation using G-CDs were determined as follows: G-CDs synthesized at 150 °C, with a G-CDs concentration of 7 mL, and at pH 11. Additionally, the most effective wavelength for this process was found to be 365 nm. The study investigated the kinetics and photogenerated charge carriers using steady-state fluorescence investigation and photoelectrochemical measurements. Furthermore, the photocatalytic mechanism of G-CDs under simulated sunlight irradiation was determined to be primarily associated with h+, •OH, and O2-. These findings highlight the potential of green-synthesized G-CDs as highly efficient main photocatalyst for environmental remediation applications.

Yasmin M. Bakier: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Haitham M. El-Bery: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.111493.
